title: Chapter 8 Quiz
description: Prove your knowledge of Object-Oriented Programming: classes, initialization, inheritance, and overriding!

questions.question: What is the #1 foundational difference between a Struct and a Class in Swift?
options: Classes can have Methods, Structs cannot.
Classes are Reference Types (shared), Structs are Value Types (photocopied).
Structs are generally banned by Apple.
questions.explanation: Structs duplicate their data when copied. Classes share the same exact data in memory.

questions.question: Because Classes are Reference Types, what happens if I create `let a = UserClass()`, set `let b = a`, and change `b.name = "Bob"`?
options: a.name instantly becomes "Bob" as well, because A and B point to the EXACT same memory.
questions.explanation: Reference Types share data. Changing the reference updates the singular source of truth.

questions.question: Swift prefers you to use Classes as your default choice for custom data.
questions.explanation: Apple strongly encourages developers to use Structs (Value Types) by default due to their incredible safety and thread-readiness. Switch to Classes only when you explicitly need shared state or inheritance.

questions.question: Unlike Structs, Classes do NOT automatically generate an 'Initializer'. Therefore, what must you explicitly create?
questions.explanation: Classes demand you formulate an explicit `init()` function to map starting data to its properties.

questions.question: If you are trapped in an `init(name: String)` method and need to interact with the Class property `var name: String`, what keyword do you use to distinguish them?
questions.explanation: The `self` keyword refers directly to the object currently being initialized or utilized.

questions.question: To allow `SubClass` to inherit all properties and methods from `SuperClass`, you use a _____ to separate their names during declaration.
questions.explanation: The colon `:` is officially used to designate protocol conformance or class inheritance (e.g., `class Dog: Animal`).

questions.question: Can a Swift Struct inherit from another Struct?
options: Yes, but only if they are the same Type.
No. Structs explicitly CANNOT use Inheritance.
questions.explanation: Inheritance is an exclusive superpower reserved entirely for Classes.

questions.question: If `class Car: Vehicle` is declared, which class gets all of the other's logic for free?
questions.explanation: The child (`Car`) inherits the traits of the parent (`Vehicle`).

questions.question: To completely replace a function that was inherited from a Superclass, you proceed the function declaration with the _____ keyword.
questions.explanation: The `override` keyword explicitly warns the compiler that you are destroying the parent's default behavior and establishing your own.

questions.question: Inside an overridden method, what is the fastest way to manually trigger the Parent's original version of the code?
questions.explanation: Calling `super` signals that you want to travel UP the hierarchy and ping the Parent's implementation.

questions.question: Because a Struct creates a "clone" of its data when modifying it, it requires the `_____` keyword on a method to change internal variables.
questions.explanation: Structs require `mutating func`. Because Classes are Reference Types, they NEVER need the `mutating` keyword! Classes change their variables freely.

questions.question: If an `init()` method successfully finishes, all of the Class's properties MUST have value (unless they were Optionals).
questions.explanation: Initialization has one massive rule: By the time `init` hits its closing bracket `}`, every unassigned variable MUST hold data.

questions.question: If `class Bird { func fly() {} }` and `class Penguin: Bird { }`. How do you stop the penguin from flying?
options: Override the fly() method inside Penguin and give it empty or error logic.
questions.explanation: If a child inherits bad behavior, `override` is your only weapon to change or neutralize it.

questions.question: In software architecture, treating distinct sub-types utilizing identical inherited Methods without breaking the code is called...
questions.explanation: Polymorphism (Many forms) allows you to store a `Dog` and a `Cat` inside an `[Animal]` array, and successfully call `.makeSound()` for all of them.

questions.question: Why is Inheritance considered somewhat dangerous as codebases grow enormous?
options: Changes to a Parent class instantly cascade and mutate thousands of Child classes, causing unexpected breaks.
questions.explanation: Tight coupling with Parent classes means a "simple" change at the top creates gigantic ripple-effects across the app.

questions.question: While creating a Class instance, writing `let` creates a constant object, preventing you from modifying ANY of its internal properties later.
questions.explanation: Because it is a Reference Type, `let x = MyClass()` only locks the POINTER. You can still cheerfully modify `x.name = "New"` because the underlying object in memory isn't chained, only the lookup reference is!

questions.question: Is `init(age: Int) { age = age }` correct syntax?
options: No, it assigns the parameter to itself. You must use `self.age = age`.
questions.explanation: Without `self`, Swift assumes you are talking exclusively about the local parameter, achieving absolutely nothing.

questions.question: What is the name of Apple's primary UI framework that aggressively relies on Class inheritance?
questions.explanation: UIKit is notorious for enormous inheritance trees (e.g. `UIButton` inherits from `UIControl`, which inherits from `UIView`, which inherits from `UIResponder`...).

questions.question: Instead of Inheritance (Classes), modern Swift heavily favors breaking behaviors into small, adoptable chunks called _____ (to be tackled in the next chapter!).
questions.explanation: Protocol-Oriented Programming (via Structs) serves as Swift's ultimate rebuttal to messy Class inheritance.

questions.question: To absolutely block any other Class from inheriting from your Parent class, you affix the _____ keyword before its declaration.
questions.explanation: Marking `final class StringManager` completely strips away its ability to be subclassed, increasing both safety and app performance.
